coredata.dc:title: Probing the food packaging applications of green carbon quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials due to their low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, high electrical conductivity, and remarkable fluorescence properties. Their tunable emission, high intensity, biocompatibility, and ability to enhance mechanical, thermal, barrier, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties make CQDs ideal for diverse applications, including innovative food packaging. This study explores the green synthesis of CQDs from agricultural waste fibres, namely jute, coconut husk, banana, and water hyacinth, designated as CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, and CQD4, respectively. The CQDs were embedded into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to fabricate composite films S1, S2, S3, and S4, which were extensively characterized for their physicochemical and functional properties. Mechanical analysis revealed significant improvements in the tensile strength and elongation at break of all composite films, particularly for the S4 film, which increased to 1.72 ± 0.002 MPa and 223.8 ± 0.535 %, respectively, compared to the pure PVA film (1.00 ± 0.007 Mpa and 111.70 ± 0.433 %). Water barrier properties were enhanced, with the S4 film exhibiting the lowest swelling index (47.28 ± 0.022 %) and solubility (30.52 ± 0.042 %) compared to pure PVA (77.89 ± 0.058 % and 65.76 ± 0.045 %, respectively). Gas barrier properties were significantly improved, with the S1 film reducing oxygen permeability (OP) by 36.44 % (2.18 ± 0.023 cm3 m−1 24 h−1 atm−1) and the S4 film exhibiting the lowest water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of 95.11 ± 0.029 g m−2 h−1. The UV-blocking efficacy of all films exceeded 89 %, with S4 achieving 92.55 %. Antimicrobial assays demonstrated that S1 and S2 effectively inhibited Bacillus cereus and Salmonella typhi. Additionally, all films exhibited biofilm inhibitory activity, with S4 showing 100 % inhibition against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. In food packaging evaluations, the S3 and S4 films extended the shelf life of plums and bananas by up to 15 and 6 days, respectively, by reducing spoilage. Cytotoxicity studies using the MTT assay revealed that S3 and S4 films had IC50 values of 279.5 μg/mL and 260.35 μg/mL, respectively, indicating low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. These findings highlight the potential of CQDs derived from agricultural waste fibres as sustainable additives with multifunctional properties, making them promising materials for active food packaging applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising nanomaterials due to their low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, high electrical conductivity, and remarkable fluorescence properties. Their tunable emission, high intensity, biocompatibility, and ability to enhance mechanical, thermal, barrier, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties make CQDs ideal for diverse applications, including innovative food packaging. This study explores the green synthesis of CQDs from agricultural waste fibres, namely jute, coconut husk, banana, and water hyacinth, designated as CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, and CQD4, respectively. The CQDs were embedded into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to fabricate composite films S1, S2, S3, and S4, which were extensively characterized for their physicochemical and functional properties. Mechanical analysis revealed significant improvements in the tensile strength and elongation at break of all composite films, particularly for the S4 film, which increased to 1.72 ± 0.002 MPa and 223.8 ± 0.535 %, respectively, compared to the pure PVA film (1.00 ± 0.007 Mpa and 111.70 ± 0.433 %). Water barrier properties were enhanced, with the S4 film exhibiting the lowest swelling index (47.28 ± 0.022 %) and solubility (30.52 ± 0.042 %) compared to pure PVA (77.89 ± 0.058 % and 65.76 ± 0.045 %, respectively). Gas barrier properties were significantly improved, with the S1 film reducing oxygen permeability (OP) by 36.44 % (2.18 ± 0.023 cm3 m−1 24 h−1 atm−1) and the S4 film exhibiting the lowest water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of 95.11 ± 0.029 g m−2 h−1. The UV-blocking efficacy of all films exceeded 89 %, with S4 achieving 92.55 %. Antimicrobial assays demonstrated that S1 and S2 effectively inhibited Bacillus cereus and Salmonella typhi. Additionally, all films exhibited biofilm inhibitory activity, with S4 showing 100 % inhibition against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus. In food packaging evaluations, the S3 and S4 films extended the shelf life of plums and bananas by up to 15 and 6 days, respectively, by reducing spoilage. Cytotoxicity studies using the MTT assay revealed that S3 and S4 films had IC50 values of 279.5 μg/mL and 260.35 μg/mL, respectively, indicating low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility. These findings highlight the potential of CQDs derived from agricultural waste fibres as sustainable additives with multifunctional properties, making them promising materials for active food packaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demand for convenient and long-lasting food products has driven advancements in food preservation and packaging technologies. Effective packaging solutions are crucial for reducing food waste and ensuring the safety and quality of food throughout the supply chain (Shen et al., 2024). The widely used traditional petroleum-based packaging materials have raised many problems due to their environmental impact and potential health risk (Yin and Woo, 2024). The interest in exploring and adopting new sustainable alternatives for these existing non-biodegradable petroleum-based packaging materials is growing day-by-day due to their adverse impact on environment and human health (D'Almeida and De Albuquerque, 2024). These alternatives aim to offer a safer, biodegradable, eco-friendly, and cost-effective solution to the challenges posed by traditional packaging materials (Chen et al., 2024).

Recent advancements in food packaging technology have led to the emergence of active packaging films, which incorporate functional materials to enhance food preservation (Raghuvanshi et al., 2023). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), with their unique properties such as biocompatibility, low toxicity, and photoluminescence, have emerged as promising candidates for developing sustainable and active food packaging materials (Sun et al., 2024; Z. Yang et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023). Integrating CQDs into polymeric matrices imparts antimicrobial, antioxidant, and UV-blocking properties to films (Hong et al., 2024; Singh et al., 2024). Moreover, CQDs enable the development of pH-sensitive films for real-time monitoring of food spoilage (Riahi et al., 2024a; Sandeep et al., 2025; Song et al., 2024; Wagh et al., 2024). These innovations offer significant potential to enhance food safety, extend shelf life, and reduce waste.

CQDs, when incorporated into polymeric matrices, effectively inhibit foodborne pathogens, thereby extending food shelf life through mechanisms such as reactive oxygen species generation, cell membrane disruption, and interference with DNA replication (Moradi et al., 2023). Recent studies highlight the antimicrobial efficacy of CQDs derived from diverse sources. For instance, Carbon quantum Dots (CQDs) integrated into thermoplastic chitosan films exhibited antimicrobial activity against L. monocytogenes and E. coli (Althawab et al., 2024). Similarly, gelatin-based films reinforced with CQDs derived from Sophora japonica extract (R-CDs) demonstrated significant antimicrobial activity against S. aureus, B. subtilis, L. monocytogenes, and B. cinerea (Guo et al., 2024). Additionally, CDs derived from syrup bottle waste (SyCDs) exhibited potent antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, the agar well diffusion method revealed that SyCDs effectively inhibited the growth of S. aureus, S. mutans, P. aeruginosa, and E. coli, with their activity against E. coli being comparable to standard antibiotics (Thirumalaivasan et al., 2024).

CQDs enhances the tensile strength and barrier performances of food packaging films (Deepika et al., 2023; Silva et al., 2023). Incorporating CQDs into polymer matrices significantly improves tensile strength, ensuring film integrity during handling, storage, and transportation (Ezati et al., 2022). Their UV-blocking, water vapor, and gas barrier properties helps to maintain food freshness, nutritional value, and quality by reducing degradation (Khoshkalampour et al., 2024; Tripathi et al., 2024). For instance, Riahi et al. (2024b) demonstrated that incorporating Zn-doped avocado-derived carbon dots (Zn-ACDs) into cellulose nanofibre-pullulan films resulted in a 45.4 % increase in tensile strength and a 64.1 % enhancement in elongation at break. These improvements were attributed to the reinforcing effects of Zn-ACDs and their ability to establish strong hydrogen bonds within the polymeric matrix (Riahi et al., 2024b). Similarly, Khoshkalampour et al. improved UV-blocking and water barrier properties by integrating CQDs into casein-tragacanth gum composite films (Khoshkalampour et al., 2024).

In addition to these studies, the CQDs have been utilized for the fabrication of biofriendly and biodegradable food packaging materials based on both natural polymers such as chitosan (Hadavifar et al., 2025; Kamanna et al., 2023; Sul et al., 2025), cellulose (Ahmadi et al., 2024; Tammina and Rhim, 2023; J. Yang et al., 2024), starch (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024; Cheng et al., 2024b; Sun et al., 2024), pectin (Shen et al., 2024), and alginate (Mao et al., 2023; Z. Yang et al., 2024) and synthetic polymers including polypropylene, polyhydroxyalkanoates (Singh et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2023), polyvinylpyrrolidone (Kalanidhi K and Nagaraaj P, 2022), and polyvinyl alcohol (Hong et al., 2025; Hou et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2024).

Most of the previous studies have focused on the functional characteristics of CQDs, such as antibacterial, antioxidant, UV-blocking, and barrier properties, essential for food packaging materials. However, a comparative analysis of CQDs derived from different agricultural waste fibres for composite films in food packaging remains lacking. This study aims to address this gap by comparing the functional properties of composite films fabricated using CQDs derived from various agricultural waste fibres including jute, coconut husk, banana, and water hyacinth. These CQDs were synthesized using a green, one-step hydrothermal method and incorporated into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to create CQD-PVA composite films. The films were evaluated for mechanical strength, water resistance, gas barrier properties, antimicrobial activity, UV-barrier performance and cytotoxicity. This research aims to identify the most effective composite films for food packaging, specifically for preserving plums and bananas.

Banana fibres were obtained from Banana Research Station, Thrissur, Kerala, India, in processed fibre form. Jute fibres were purchased from a local market of Ernakulam, Kerala, India. Coconut fibres were sourced from Ernakulam, Kerala, India, and sun-dried for two weeks before being ground into powder. Water hyacinth plants were collected from a local water body in Ernakulam, Kerala, India, and only the stem part was cleaned, sun-dried for two weeks, and crushed into a powder form.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized from natural fibres including jute, coconut husk, banana, and water hyacinth fibre powder following the method of Khan et al. with slight modifications (Khan et al., 2023). For this 0.25 g of each fibre powder was dispersed in 25 mL of water with gentle stirring for 30 min and the solution mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature (30 °C ± 2) and then reaction solutions were filtered using Whatman 1filter paper (1 μm pore size, 90 mm diameter) and centrifuged for 15 min at 800 rpm. The synthesized CQDs were then purified using column chromatography technique and the obtained CQDs solutions of jute, coconut husk, banana, and water hyacinth fibre powder were named as CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, CQD4, respectively. All the synthesized CQDs were stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C before further testing and characterization.

CQDs- PVA composite films were fabricated by solution casting method. First, 5 g (5 %, w/v) of PVA pellets were dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water. Then, CQD solutions were added in a 1:1 ratio to the PVA solution and stirred at 500 rpm for 10 min using a Neuation iStir HP320. Finally, 20 mL of the prepared film-forming solution was poured into glass Petri dish and dried for 24 h at 60 °C in vacuum oven to obtain CQDs-PVA based composite films (Fig. 2). The same procedure was followed for the fabrication of PVA composite films using all the four carbon quantum dots CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, CQD4, and the resulting films were named as S1, S2, S3, and S4, respectively.

The morphology and structure of the prepared CQDs (CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, CQD4) were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM 2100, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The fluorescence and UV -Visible spectra of the synthesized CQDs were measured with a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon GmbH, München, Germany) and UV–Vis (UV-1800 Shimadzu, Japan) spectrometer, respectively. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CQDs was collected on a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer, USA. Raman spectra of synthesized CQDs were obtained from Confocal Raman Microscope with AFM (Alpha300RA AFM & RAMAN, WITec GmbH, Ulm, Germany). The surface morphology of CQDs-PVA composite films were studied using Scanning electron microscopy (SEM-EDAX: Jeol 6390LA/OXFORD XMX N). The UV–Vis spectra of CQDs-PVA composite films were recorded using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Lambda 750, USA). The mechanical properties of CQDs-PVA composite films were evaluated using Universal Testing Machine (UTM) (AG1, Shimadzu, Japan) at a stretch rate of 400 mm/min. Samples measuring 1 × 8 cm2 of different composite films were tested, and the resulting data were plotted as stress-strain curves.
Water solubility, moisture content and swelling index of the fabricated composite films were determined using a standard procedure (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024).

The biodegradability of the CQDs-PVA composite film was evaluated by the soil burial method (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024). Samples of each composite film, measuring 1 × 1 cm2, were initially weighed and then buried in moist garden soil. Over a 50-day period, the samples were regularly removed from the soil every 5 days, weighed, and the percentage of weight loss was determined using the formula given below.

The Water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) and oxygen permeability (OP) of the prepared CQDs-PVA composite films was evaluated using a previously reported method with slight modifications (Amaregouda and Kamanna, 2023).

To determine the WVTR, 10 mL of deionized water was added to a glass bottle with an internal diameter of 30 mm. The bottle mouth was securely sealed using a rubber band and tightly wrapped with the test film. The initial weight of the bottle was recorded before placing it in an oven at 40 °C for 24 h. After the designated period, the bottle was removed from the oven and reweighed. The WVTR of the film was then calculated using the following equation:

To determine OP, a 30 mL bottle with an opening radius of 1.5 cm was covered and sealed using the prepared film. The bottle was placed in a desiccator at room temperature, and its weight was recorded daily for three days. The weight change over time was analyzed using linear regression (R2 > 0.99), and the slope of the resulting line was calculated using Equations (6) and (7):

Here, ΔP represents the difference in partial vapor pressure between pure water and a dry atmosphere (0.02308 atm at 25 °C), and L denotes the average film thickness. The area of the film was calculated based on the radius of the bottle's opening. The OP of all the CQDs-PVA composite films and pure PVA was calculated to compare their oxygen barrier properties.

The antimicrobial properties of the CQDs- PVA composite films were evaluated using the disc diffusion method against various bacterial pathogens. The tested organisms included Gram-negative bacteria (Shigella flexneri, E coli and Salmonella T yphi) and Gram-positive bacteria (Bacillus cereus and Staphylococcus aureus). Polymer film discs, 6 mm in diameter, were placed on the surface of Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA) medium inoculated with the respective bacterial strains. Prior to testing, the discs were sterilized by UV light exposure for 30 min, and sterility was verified by incubating the UV-treated membranes in liquid LB medium for 24 h. An overnight culture (100 μL) of each test organism was spread evenly over the MHA surface and allowed to dry for 10 min. The polymer discs were then placed on the agar surface, and the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Antimicrobial activity was assessed by examining the inhibition zones, indicating the discs' ability to prevent bacterial growth.

The biofilm inhibition activity of the CQDs- PVA films was evaluated using a crystal violet assay with two test organisms: E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. The surface-sterilized polymer films were incubated with bacterial solutions as described in the assay protocol of Alaş et al. (2022) After a 4-day incubation period, the films were stained with 0.1 % crystal violet for 30 min. Excess crystal violet was removed through extensive washing with distilled water. The degree of biofilm inhibition was quantified by measuring the reduction in absorbance of the dye at 585 nm, comparing the treated membranes to the control (untreated membrane).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Food packaging applications of CQDs- PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The potential of the CQDs-PVA composite films for antimicrobial food packaging material and shelf-life extension were evaluated. Among the prepared films, S3 and S4 exhibited superior performance in terms of mechanical strength, UV barrier properties, water solubility, biofilm inhibition activity and antibacterial activity. Consequently, only S3 and S4 were selected for further evaluation in food packaging applications.

To evaluate the efficacy of these composite films as antimicrobial material, both packaging and coating studies were conducted using plums (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024; Hou et al., 2024). Plums of uniform size, shape, color, and ripeness were selected. For packaging study, partially ripened plums were covered with S3, S4 and pure PVA films. The unwrapped plums were used as controls. In the coating study, fully ripened plums were immersed in S3, S4, and PVA film solutions for 1–2 min and then air-dried. Uncoated plums were served as controls. Three plum samples were studied for each treatment group in both the packaging and coating experiments. The plum samples were stored at room temperature (30 °C ± 2) for 15 days. Digital images of randomly selected samples were captured every three days to monitor visual changes in the plums.

To assess the shelf-life extension properties of CQDs-PVA composite solutions, bananas were coated with S3, S4, and pure PVA film-forming solutions using a physical brushing method (Zhao et al., 2022). Uncoated bananas served as controls. Three samples were studied for each treatment group, and all samples were stored at room temperature (30 °C ± 2) for six days. Visual changes were monitored using digital images captured at two-day intervals.

An in vitro method, known as the MTT assay (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide), was utilized to examine the cytotoxicity of synthesized CQDs. CHO-K1 (Chinese Hamster Ovary) cell lines were obtained from the National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS), Pune, India. A 200 μL cell suspension was seeded into a 96-well plate at the required cell density (1 × 104 cells/well) without the testing agent. After 24 h, the growth medium was removed, and different concentrations of S3 and S4 composite solutions (100, 50, 25, and 12.5 μg/mL) in DMEM were added in triplicates to the respective wells, followed by incubation at 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator. Cells treated with 20 μL of sterile distilled water served as the control. After 48 h of incubation, the sample content in the wells was removed, and 20 μL of MTT solution (prepared from a stock concentration of 5 mg/mL) was added to all test and control wells, followed by incubation in the dark for 4 h. After the incubation period, the MTT reagent was removed, and 200 μL of solubilization solution (DMSO) was added. The absorbance values were measured using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 560 nm (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan).

The percentage of cell viability was calculated using the formula:
(8)

All measurements were performed in triplicate, and the results are expressed as mean ± SD (standard deviation). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was conducted to analyze the data, and the significance of each mean value was evaluated (p < 0.05) using Tukey's Post-Hoc Test in the statistical software jamovi (Version 2.5; The jamovi project, 2024, Sydney, Australia).

In this study, CQDs were synthesized from agricultural waste fibres, including jute, coconut husk, banana, and water hyacinth, via a one-step hydrothermal method. Fig. 1
illustrates the schematic diagram of CQD preparation using the hydrothermal method. All CQDs derived from these fibres exhibited blue luminescence under UV light and were characterised using various spectroscopic techniques. PVA-CQDs composite films were then fabricated by incorporating each type of CQD into a PVA matrix through the solvent casting method (Fig. 2). Four composite films, each containing CQDs from a different agricultural fibre, were prepared, and their functional properties were studied and compared with those of the pure PVA film.

The microstructure of CQDs derived from natural fibres such as banana, jute, coconut husk, and water hyacinth were analyzed using TEM, as shown in Fig. 3 . The TEM analysis confirmed that the CQDs were well-dispersed, formed monodisperse spheres within the quantum size range (below 10 nm), and exhibited no agglomeration. The size distribution of the different CQDs were further analyzed by plotting histograms, as depicted in Fig. 4 .

The results indicated that the average size of the CQDs was as follows: CQD1, approximately 5.21 ± 0.21 nm; CQD2, approximately 5.92 ± 0.23 nm; CQD4, approximately 4.75 ± 0.26 nm; and CQD3, with the smallest size at approximately 4.02 ± 0.34 nm. These values were determined by fitting the size distribution histograms to a Gaussian model.

FT-IR measurements were performed on all synthesized CQDs to analyze their surface functional groups, and the results were compared (Fig. 5 a). The FTIR spectra of CQD1 and CQD2 showed overlapping broad bands at 3330.45 cm−1 and 3328.74 cm−1 respectively, attributed to –NH and –OH stretching vibrations (D. Yang et al., 2023). Both CQD1 and CQD2 also exhibited sharp peaks at 1636.16 cm−1 and 1636.04 cm−1 respectively, corresponding to C=O stretching vibrations (Khan et al., 2023). Similarly, CQD3 displayed a broad peak at 3327.36 cm−1 and a sharp peak at 1635.87 cm−1, while CQD4 showed peaks at 3328.16 cm−1 and 1636.11 cm−1, confirming the presence of –NH, –OH and C=O groups. The C=O stretching vibration, resulting from the oxidation of the C–OH bond during the hydrothermal reaction, serves as a luminescent emission source suitable for fluorescence detection (Huang et al., 2021). The FTIR results indicate that the surface interactions between these CQDs and the PVA polymer matrix are facilitated through weak interactions, such as hydrogen bonding (Wagh et al., 2024).

The Raman spectroscopy analysis depicted in Fig. 5b reveals the distinctive D and G bands present in all examined CQDs. These bands correspond to the disordered graphitic lattice and the breathing and stretching vibrational modes of the sp2 carbon domains, respectively (Zhang et al., 2024). The D bands were identified at 1357 cm−1, 1349 cm−1, 1361 cm−1, and 1353 cm−1 for CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, and CQD4, respectively. Correspondingly, the G band signals were observed at 1573 cm−1, 1574 cm−1, 1585 cm−1, and 1581 cm−1 for CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, and CQD4, respectively (Xu et al., 2024). Notably, in the cases of CQD1 and CQD2, an additional peak at 2608 cm−1 and 2524 cm−1, respectively, attributed to the two-dimensional band (Shereema et al., 2015). The calculated ID/IG ratios were determined as ∼0.70 for all CQDs. The ID/IG ratio obtained in our analysis suggests the presence of a graphitic core crystalline structure within the CQDs, as seen in numerous previous studies (Atchudan et al., 2021; Daniel, 2023; Zhang et al., 2024). Moreover, this ratio serves as a qualitative indicator of the degree of disorder within structural defects after oxidation (Daniel, 2023; Zhang et al., 2024).

The photosensitive capabilities of CQDs have been identified as the most advantageous and extensively researched aspect. The optical characteristics of CQDs are predominantly examined through UV–vis spectroscopy and PL spectroscopy.

The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 5c) of the produced CQDs differ from one another based on the synthesis methods used and the starting materials utilized during preparation (Daniel, 2023). The synthesized carbon dots are light brown, and dark brown under day light irradiation but it showed blue luminescence on exposure of UV light as shown in the inset of Fig. 5(d–g). Similarly, the composite films exhibit a light brown colour under daylight while showing blue luminescence under UV light, as illustrated in Fig. 5(h–k). The UV–vis absorbance maxima of CQD1 was observed at 310 nm, while that of CQD3 was appeared at 278 nm, both indicating the presence of n → π∗ transitions due to the presence of C=O group. Interestingly, CD3 and CD2 demonstrated spectral overlap, resulting in a broad peak centered at approximately 275 nm. This is attributed to n → π∗ transitions of the C=O and π → π∗ transition of the conjugated C=C bands (Khoshkalampour et al., 2024; Liu, 2020; Zhu et al., 2023). The UV transmittance properties of these CQDs underscore their potential for use in the development of UV-blocking films (Wagh et al., 2024).

The emission wavelength and size-dependent photoluminescent characteristics are the hallmark of CQDs. From both a fundamental and applied perspective, photoluminescence is considered as one of the most captivating behaviors exhibited by these nanoparticles. The PL spectra of all CQDs with variation of excitation wavelength (320–380 nm) are shown in Fig. 6 (a–d). All the CQDs exhibited emission maxima at 360 nm excitation wavelength, with emission peaks at 445 nm, 444 nm, 437 nm, and 453 nm in CQD1, CQD2, CQD3, and CQD4, respectively, indicating a strong blue luminescence emission (Daniel, 2023; Khan et al., 2024). The emission maxima of different CQDs remain almost the same regardless of variations in excitation wavelength, suggesting the absence of additional impurities and relatively uniform size and surface state of CQDs (Daniel, 2022, 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and functional properties of CQDs-PVA composite films

ce:section.ce:para: According to the micro-morphology analysis presented in Fig. 7 a–d, the SEM images reveal distinct surface characteristics of the CQDs- PVA composite films. The surface of the S1 film appears flat, uniform, and compact without any evident cavities, cracks, or lumps (Fig. 10a), indicating excellent compatibility between the PVA matrix and CQD1 (J. Yang et al., 2024). However, upon incorporation of CQD2 in to PVA matrix, Fig. 10b displays surface heterogeneity, with the presence of granular substances, identified as CQD2. It is evident that CQDs can accumulate in specific regions of the film, resulting in a slight increase in surface roughness attributed to nanoparticle proliferation (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024). Among all the CQDs-PVA composite films, S3 exhibited relatively uniform surface micro-morphology. By contrast, a large number of granular substances appeared on the S2 film surface, attributing to the aggregation of CQD4 at high content resulting in elevated surface roughness (Mao et al., 2023). The incorporation of these CQDs into the PVA matrix film did not result in the formation of surface cracks, voids or cavities, confirming their compatibility within the PVA matrix. The enhanced compatibility and uniform distribution of CQDs can be attributed to the potential formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the CQDs and the PVA matrix, resulting in the development of dense and cohesive films (Wagh et al., 2024).

To prevent UV-induced oxidation and rancidity in food, effective UV-shielding packaging materials are crucial (Silva et al., 2023). The UV-blocking performance of CQDs-PVA composite films was evaluated using a UV spectrum analyser. As shown in Fig. 8 a, pure PVA films exhibited a maximum UV transmittance of 34.53 ± 0.026 % (200–400 nm), corresponding to a UV-blocking capacity of 65.4 ± 0.014 %. However, the incorporation of CQDs significantly enhanced UV-shielding properties. S1 and S3 films demonstrated nearly identical UV transmittances of 10.63 ± 0.025 % and 10.62 ± 0.015 %, respectively, with a corresponding UV-blocking capacity of approximately 89.4 ± 0.015 %. Notably, the S2 film exhibited a slightly higher UV-blocking capacity of 90.47 ± 0.041 % due to its lower UV transmittance of 9.53 ± 0.039 %. The S4 film exhibited the highest UV-blocking capacity of 92.55 ± 0.012 % with a UV transmittance of only 7.45 ± 0.018 %. These results indicate that incorporating CQDs into PVA films significantly enhances their UV-shielding properties, making them effective barriers against UV light. This improvement is essential for preserving food quality by protecting organoleptic attributes such as odour, colour, and flavour, as well as maintaining nutritional value (Hong et al., 2024; Murugan et al., 2024b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water resistance properties and biodegradability of PVA-composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Water resistance properties of the packaging films are crucial to avoid food from moisture-induced spoilage. This study evaluated the water resistance of CQDs-PVA composite films by assessing their water solubility (WS), moisture content (MC), swelling index (SI) and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR). As depicted in Table 1 , the significant reduction in the in WS, MC, SI, and WVTR values indicate that the addition of CQDs into PVA matrix enhances water resistance properties of composite films. Among the four composite films, S4 exhibited the highest water resistance, followed by S3 and S2, while S1 demonstrated the lowest. The CQDs ability to hinder the penetration of water molecules through the polymer matrix, enhances the water resistance properties of the CQDs-PVA composite films than that of pure PVA film, without CQDs (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024). The addition of CQDs reduces the number of hydroxyl groups in PVA matrix, which diminishes the capacity to form hydrogen bond with the water and this reduction in hydrophilicity led to decreased water solubility and swelling. Furthermore, the increased rigidity and reduced mobility of PVA, induced by the addition of CQDs, further suppressed swelling. The formation of strong hydrogen bonds between CQDs and PVA also contributed to the enhanced water resistance by reducing the material's hydrophilic character. While the CQDs-PVA composite films exhibited excellent water resistance, it is important to note that the residual hydroxyl groups in PVA can still facilitate limited water absorption, leading to a slight degree of swelling (El-Shamy and Zayied, 2020).

The biodegradability of the synthesized CQDs-PVA composite films was evaluated over a 50-day period using a soil burial method. As depicted in Fig. 8b, the pure PVA film and S1 film exhibited comparable biodegradability. The S1 film exhibited the highest biodegradability followed by S2, while the S3 and S4 film exhibited lower biodegradability, due to their enhanced antimicrobial activity and water resistance properties. The degradation of these composite films primarily occurs through hydrolysis process. Water facilitates microbial activity, leading to the breakdown of the polymer structure. Additionally, water molecules help to break the hydrogen bonds within the polymer chains, resulting in the formation of smaller fragments (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024). This explains why films with higher water solubility tend to degrade more readily compared to those with lower water solubility. The degradation rate of each composite film is influenced by its specific composition and properties. All the PVA- CQDs composite films exhibited moderate biodegradability, these findings suggest that the fabricated composite films offer a promising and environmentally friendly solution for food packaging.

The gaseous composition inside the packaging significantly influences the physiological metabolism of fruits and vegetables, playing a vital role in controlling decay processes. The extended preservation of these perishable items is achieved through an optimal gas environment with reduced oxygen levels and increased carbon dioxide concentrations. This balanced atmosphere helps lower their respiratory rate and suppress microbial growth, effectively extending their shelf life (Althawab et al., 2024). The oxygen permeability (OP) and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) of the prepared CQDs-PVA composite films are presented in Table 1. The pure PVA film exhibited an OP value of 3.43 ± 0.035 cm3 m−1 24 h−1 atm−1 and a WVTR of 118.89 ± 0.021 g m−2 h−1. However, the incorporation of CQDs into the composite films significantly enhanced their barrier properties. Among the CQDs-PVA films, the S1 film exhibited the lowest OP value (2.18 ± 0.023 cm3 m−1 24 h−1 atm−1), representing a 36.44 % reduction compared to pure PVA, while the S4 film displayed the lowest WVTR (95.11 ± 0.029 g m−2 h−1), indicating improved moisture resistance.

This improvement in gas barrier performance can be attributed to the role of CQDs as physical crosslinking agents, which enhance the density and compactness of the PVA matrix (Althawab et al., 2024). The uniform dispersion of CQDs creates tortuous pathways that hinder the diffusion of oxygen and water molecules, effectively reducing permeability (J. Yang et al., 2024). Additionally, hydrogen bonding interactions between CQDs and the PVA matrix contribute to the structural integrity of the film, further limiting oxygen and water vapor transmission (Wang et al., 2024). These findings indicate that CQDs-PVA composite films offer superior protective properties, making them promising materials for food packaging applications requiring extended shelf life and controlled gaseous exchange.

Assessing the mechanical properties of packaging films is crucial for determining their ability to withstand physical damage during transportation, storage, and packaging processes. Table 2 presents the thickness, tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and elasticity modulus (EM) of CQDs-PVA composite films and PVA film. The pure PVA film has a thickness of 0.16 mm, and the addition of CQDs has varying effects on film thickness. The incorporation of CQDs in the S2, S3, and S4 films significantly increased thickness due to the higher solid content in the film-forming solution. However, the thickness of the S1 film, which contains CQD1, did not differ from that of the pure PVA film. This may be related to the small particle size of CQD1 and its uniform distribution within the matrix, as confirmed by SEM image (Sun et al., 2024). CQDs aggregation was observed in the SEM images of S2 and S4 films, resulting in the formation of thicker films.

The stress-strain graph and the comparison between TS and EB values of pure PVA film and CQDs- PVA composite films are shown in Fig. 8(c and d). The addition of CQDs led to improvements in both the TS and EB values, as well as an increase in the strain values of all the CQDs/PVA films compared to the pure PVA film. The TS values of S3 and S4 films increased by 50 % and 72 % respectively, compared to the pure PVA film. Additionally, the EB values showed increases of 97.21 % and 112.1 % for S3 and S4 composite films, respectively, compared to the pure PVA film. There are several unique characteristics of CDs that contribute to the enhancement of the mechanical properties of polymer films. Due to their high surface area, CDs can physically adsorb onto the polymer macrochains, thereby reinforcing the film structure. Additionally, CDs promote the formation of a more compact and uniform polymer matrix, which enhances its mechanical integrity (Althawab et al., 2024). Furthermore, the PVA bind tightly to the surface of the CDs, and the CDs also create physical crosslinking points within the PVA matrix, further contributing to the improved mechanical properties (Althawab et al., 2024; Hou et al., 2024). The presence of abundant -OH and –NH groups on CQDs facilitates the formation of hydrogen bonding with the –OH/–COOH groups in the PVA matrix, resulting in increased crosslinking rigidity of the composite films (Hou et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2022). Interestingly, S1 and S2 exhibited only a small increase in both TS and EB values compared to pure PVA film. The lower EM values observed in all CQDs-PVA films could be due to the constrained mobility of polymer molecular chains caused by the inclusion of rigid CQDs nanoparticles (Zhao et al., 2022).

The antimicrobial activity of the CQDs-PVA composite films was evaluated against various bacterial species (Fig. 9
). Films S1 and S2 exhibited strong activity against Bacillus cereus, with no visible growth observed. In contrast, the films, S3, and S4, displayed hazy bacterial growth underneath. Composite film S1 demonstrated lower activity against Escherichia coli to the other films. Shigella flexneri and Staphylococcus aureus showed growth in the presence of all the tested films. However, film S1 was effective against Salmonella typhi, inhibiting its growth.

The antimicrobial properties of CQDs-incorporated composite films were evaluated through disk diffusion assay against various bacterial pathogens. The results indicate differential efficacy of the films against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Films S1 and S2 demonstrated robust activity against Bacillus cereus, as evidenced by the complete absence of bacterial growth under these films. This suggests that the incorporation of CQDs in these films significantly enhances their antimicrobial properties against Gram-positive bacteria. Conversely, the films S3 and S4 exhibited hazy growth, indicating limited or no antimicrobial activity. Polymer film S1 showed lower efficacy against E. coli compared to other films, highlighting a potential limitation in its spectrum of activity. The presence of growth in Shigella and Staphylococcus across all films suggests that these particular bacterial strains possess inherent resistance mechanisms that render the nanoparticle incorporation less effective. Interestingly, film S1 was effective against Salmonella, demonstrating selective antimicrobial activity that warrants further investigation into the specific interactions between the nanoparticles and the bacterial cell walls of different strains.

These findings are consistent with previous studies that have demonstrated the enhanced antimicrobial properties of nanoparticle-incorporated polymer films. For example, Yang et al. (D. Yang et al., 2023) demonstrated that incorporating silver nanoparticles loaded on chitosan and incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol to create novel food packaging films exhibited significant antimicrobial activity against Gram-negative E. coli, comparable to the findings for films S1 and S2 against Bacillus cereus in this study. Additionally, Kamanna et al. (2023) reported that chitosan/polyvinyl alcohol-based nanocomposite films incorporated with L-Glu surface-functionalized ZnO nanoparticles effectively inhibited the growth of S. aureus (Gram-positive) and E. coli (Gram-negative) bacteria, similar to the findings for films S1 and S2 in this study.
The inhibition percentages of bacterial biofilm formation on each membrane, compared with the control, are summarized in Table 3 .
These results indicate varying degrees of biofilm inhibition among the different polymer films, with S4 showing the highest inhibition against both E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 10).

The crystal violet assay results revealed significant variations in biofilm inhibition among the different composite films. The data demonstrate that film S1 has moderate activity against E. coli (15.3 %) and significant activity against S. aureus (50.7 %). Film S2 shows a substantial increase in inhibition for both E. coli (85.3 %) and S. aureus (75.3 %), indicating a superior formulation in combating biofilm formation. Films S3 and S4 exhibit near-complete to complete inhibition of biofilms for both bacterial species, with S4 achieving 100 % inhibition, suggesting it is the most effective film among those tested.

Our findings align with those of other studies that have investigated the biofilm inhibition capabilities of nanoparticle-incorporated films. For instance, a study by Hosnedlova et al. (2022) demonstrated that silver nanoparticles significantly inhibited biofilm formation by both E. coli and S. aureus, comparable to the high inhibition rates observed with films S3 and S4. Similarly, da Silva et al. (Da Silva et al., 2024) found that biologically synthesized silver nanoparticles were highly effective in preventing biofilm formation by S. aureus. The differential activity of the nanoparticle-incorporated polymer films against various bacterial strains and biofilms highlights the potential for these materials to be tailored for specific antimicrobial applications. The high efficacy of films S3 and S4, in particular, suggests that further optimization and testing could lead to the development of highly effective antimicrobial surfaces for medical and industrial applications.

The incorporation of CQDs presents a promising avenue for enhancing the biofilm inhibition properties of polymer films, potentially offering a robust solution to the growing issue of bacterial resistance.

The functional properties of CQDs-PVA composite films were systematically compared with recent literature, as summarized in Table 4 . Our findings reveal that the antimicrobial efficacy, water resistance, and shelf-life properties of our composite films were either comparable to or superior to those reported in recent studies on CQDs-PVA films, highlighting their strong potential for food packaging applications. Furthermore, while prior works have primarily focused on the fundamental aspects of CQDs-PVA composites, our study uniquely explores biodegradability and antibiofilm inhibition properties, which have not been previously discussed. Notably, our composite films exhibited outstanding performance in these areas, reinforcing their sustainability and antifouling capabilities. Additionally, oxygen permeability studies, which are rarely addressed in recent literature, were comprehensively analyzed in this work, providing valuable insights into the barrier properties of the films. Among the prepared films, S3 and S4 exhibited superior performance in terms of mechanical strength, UV barrier properties, water solubility, biofilm inhibition activity, and antibacterial activity, as determined by comparing our results with recent literature. Consequently, only S3 and S4 were selected for further evaluation in food packaging applications using plums and bananas. Moreover, cytotoxicity assessments were conducted on S3 and S4 to ensure their safety before their application in real food studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of films as food packaging material

ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating the cytotoxicity of packaging materials is essential to determine their suitability for food applications. In this study, the cytotoxicity of the S3 and S4 composite film solutions was assessed using the MTT assay, as these films demonstrated superior functional properties and were selected for food packaging applications in plums and bananas. The in vitro cytotoxicity results, presented in Table 5 , indicate that the S3 and S4 composite film solutions had minimal impact on CHO-K1 cell viability, with IC50 values of 279.5 μg/mL and 260.35 μg/mL, respectively. Fig. 11 presents images of the cytotoxicity studies conducted for the control (a) and the S3 and S4 composite film solutions at various concentrations (b–i). The relatively high IC50 values suggest low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility of the composite solutions. These findings indicate that the S3 and S4 composite films are promising candidates for safe food packaging applications though further in vivo evaluations may be required to comprehensively assess their biological safety.
A plum storage study was conducted to assess the antimicrobial effectiveness of S3 and S4 composite films as active packaging and coating materials. The observations are given in Fig. 12 a-b.

In coating study (Fig. 12a), fully ripened plums were used and all plums initially had a fresh appearance, after three days of storage, the control sample showed green and black mildew appearance. On the 6th day of storage sample coated with pure PVA film began to display two noticeable green and white mildew appearance, with various mouldy spots becoming apparent. The width of mould in the control sample grows further. Plums coated with S3 and S4 solution showed no signs of mildew growth on the surface of the plum even after storage for 11 days. However, on the 12th day the mildew growth started to appear on the plum coated with S3 solution and plump coated with S4 solution retained the freshness up to 15 days without any mould growth.

Partially ripened plums were selected for packaging evaluation. As illustrated in Fig. 12b, all plums exhibited initial uniformity in ripeness and freshness. While all plums, except those wrapped in S3 film, progressed to full maturity by the third day of storage, S4-packaged plums remained in the pre-ripening stage until the sixth day. Post-maturation, the control group exhibited a burst and a small green mould spot on the ninth day of storage. Conversely, plums stored in PVA became more softened and ruptured on the same day. By the twelfth day, mould growth on the control group had intensified, accompanied by the emergence of white mould on the PVA-wrapped plums. Additionally, a foul-smelling, liquid discharge was observed from the ruptured PVA-wrapped plums. In contrast, S3 and S4-wrapped plums remained free from contamination or mould growth throughout the storage period. On the fifteenth day, half of the control group exhibited mould growth, and the leakage from PVA-wrapped plums became more pronounced. While the S3-wrapped plums developed a minor mould spot, S4-wrapped plums maintained their initial freshness until the eighteenth day.

To evaluate the efficacy of S3 and S4 composite films in extending the shelf life of bananas, a storage experiment was performed. As depicted in Fig. 12c, all bananas initially presented a uniform, yellow exterior with minimal blemishes. However, during storage, the bananas underwent a natural ripening process, culminating in decay characterized by the development of black spots. Uncoated bananas exhibited the most rapid deterioration, becoming commercially unviable within six days due to extensive blackening. While PVA-coated bananas also developed black spots, their overall appearance remained notably superior to uncoated bananas. Importantly, bananas treated with the S4 solution demonstrated a significantly lower incidence of black spots compared to those treated with S3, thereby maintaining their commercial value for a longer duration. The results of this storage experiment indicate that S3 and S4 composite films effectively extend the shelf life of bananas, primarily due to their enhanced antimicrobial properties.

This study successfully demonstrated a green and sustainable approach to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from agricultural waste fibres such as jute, coconut husk, banana, and water hyacinths. Employing a one-step hydrothermal carbonization method, we obtained water-soluble, monodisperse CQDs with exceptional fluorescence properties. By incorporating these CQDs PVA matrices, we developed composite films exhibiting enhanced mechanical properties, antimicrobial activity, biofilm inhibition capacity, UV-blocking capabilities, water resistance, and essential attributes for effective food packaging materials. In addition to these properties, the composite films exhibited improved gas barrier performance, reducing oxygen and moisture permeability, which is crucial for maintaining food quality and extending shelf life. Furthermore, cytotoxicity studies confirmed the biocompatibility of the S3 and S4 films, ensuring their safety for food contact applications.

Food packaging experiments revealed the efficacy of the S3 and S4 composite films in extending the shelf life of perishable fruits like plums and bananas. These films effectively maintained freshness and prevented spoilage, even under ambient conditions. This research highlights the potential of integrating CQDs derived from renewable agricultural waste resources into PVA films for sustainable and efficient food packaging applications. The promising outcomes underscore the need for further investigations to optimize these formulations and explore their effectiveness in diverse food preservation scenarios, ultimately contributing to a reduction in food waste and enhancement of food safety during storage and distribution.

Fathima C. S: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Akash M. Murali: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Sunish K. S: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration. Sobhi Daniel: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.
